title: Quantum Optical Soliton Dynamics Beyond Linearization: An Open-System Approach
authors: Chris Gustin, Ryotatsu Yangimoto, Edwin Ng, Hideo Mabuchi
abstract: We introduce two approaches to modeling the quantum dynamics of optical $χ^{(3)}$ solitons. Taking an open-system viewpoint, we project the underlying quantum field into system (soliton) and residual reservoir components. The reservoir is treated as either (i) a discrete ``Lanczos supermode'' (LSM) expansion which localizes dynamics to a few-supermode basis, or (ii) a non-local environment which can be traced out by deriving a Markovian master equation (ME). Using these methods, we analyze and identify the quantum structure of both the soliton's stability and its hierarchy of perturbations. Through numerical simulations, we confirm both methods effectively capture quantum-induced soliton phase shifts in a concise few-mode (single-mode for ME) basis, and the LSM approach also captures photon loss which arises from non-Markovian dispersive couplings. As neither method is limited to the linearized regime, our approaches provide powerful computational tools to analyze complex non-Gaussian quantum dynamics of solitons where other commonly-used methods fail, providing insight into such non-perturbative regimes. We also investigate radiation that occurs in the presence of higher-order dispersion with ultrashort pulses, deriving a ME that predicts photon loss consistent with classical theory, but find that both classical and ME theory dramatically underestimate the actual amount of dissipation, which we explain in terms of dispersive coupling-induced soliton broadening.
summary: This paper introduces new theoretical methods to model the quantum evolution of optical solitons beyond the standard linearized regime. By treating the soliton as an open system, the authors successfully capture complex non-Gaussian dynamics and explain why traditional theories underestimate photon loss in the presence of high-order dispersion.
main_problem: Modeling the quantum dynamics of optical chi(3) solitons in non-perturbative, non-Gaussian regimes where traditional linearization-based methods fail.
main_result: The authors develop two effective approaches (LSM and ME) that capture quantum-induced phase shifts and photon loss, and identify that dispersive-coupling-induced soliton broadening causes significant underestimation of dissipation in standard theories.
method: Two approaches: a discrete Lanczos Supermode (LSM) expansion and a continuous Markovian Master Equation (ME) derived via a Born-Markov approximation.
model_or_system: A quantum chi(3) nonlinear waveguide system described by a field Hamiltonian containing quadratic dispersion and Kerr nonlinearity, treated as an open system where the field is partitioned into a soliton component and a residual reservoir.
key_observables: Soliton phase shifts, photon loss (dissipation/evaporation), soliton stability, and quantum-induced soliton diffusion.
important_parameters: Photon number (mesoscopic regime), nonlinearity strength, group velocity dispersion (GVD), and third-order dispersion (TOD).
main_assumptions: The ME approach assumes a Markovian reservoir, while the LSM approach assumes dynamics can be localized to a few-supermode basis.
figures_summary: Schematic of field partitioning; plots of ME dissipator matrix elements; Wigner function showing non-classical negativity; and comparisons of photon loss rates across LSM, ME, and GSSF models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the open-system framework, details the LSM and ME formalisms, validates them against MPS benchmarks, analyzes stability and phase shifts, and investigates higher-order dispersion effects.
why_interesting: It provides powerful computational tools for analyzing non-Gaussian, non-perturbative dynamics in open quantum systems, specifically addressing the breakdown of linearization in mesoscopic bosonic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of quantum soliton (a) continuum model described by Eq. (1) and (b,c) our reduced approaches using the ME and LSM formalisms, respectively. In (a), the zoom- in shows the self-phase modulation (SPM) self-interactions and group velocity dispersion (GVD) local interactions expe- rienced at each position z along the waveform. In (b,c), the soliton subspace couples to an effective reservoir, which can be represented either as a (b) non-local continuum (ME ap- proach) or (c) as a finite set of LSMs. The ME continuum op- erators ˆc† z create spatially distributed single-photon wavefunc- tions (with a soliton “hole”) ψ′ 0(z) = δ(z −z0) −u0(z0)u0(z). Figure 3. Real (red) and imaginary (blue) components of the diagonal of the ME dissipator matrix Λnn for (a) ¯n = 10 and (b) ¯n = 1000. In (b) the approximate form which gives the Hamiltonian ˆHeff = ˆH0 + ˆHfluc is shown as dashed lines (omitting the Ω¯n contribution, which arises from L2). The dashed gray lines specify the bounds of one standard deviation ±√¯n of the initial coherent state photon distribution.

Figure 4. Illustration of LSM interactions. Dominant GVD- induced linear couplings locally connect supermodes with same parity. In the mean-field, the nonlinear (in the soliton supermode operators) coupling between n = 0 (soliton) and n = 2 LSMs destructively interferes with the GVD-induced coupling to give ˆV1. Quantum-induced soliton perturbation and evaporation via PFWM is dominantly through the 2- reservoir-photon coupling ˆV2 as well as through higher-order ˆVothers, terms which are negligible for larger ¯n.

Figure 6. Simulations of a quantum χ(3) soliton with ¯n = 5. (a) Photon population in the soliton supermode as a function of time for LSM (with varying supermode number; see labels) and MPS theory; the dashed red line corresponds to the 5 LSM model with ˆV2 set to zero. (b) Change in the Fock state probability amplitude in the reduced m = 0 density matrix at time t = 1.4T0 compared with t = 0 for 5 LSMs and MPS.
